subject: ap-psychology
theme: Developmental Psychology: Change Across the Lifespan

core_concepts.title: Prenatal Development and Critical Periods
core_concepts.summary: Development begins at conception. The embryonic period (weeks 2-8) is when major organ systems form and the embryo is most vulnerable to teratogens — environmental agents (alcohol, drugs, viruses, radiation) that disrupt prenatal development. Fetal alcohol syndrome (FAS) is a leading preventable cause of intellectual disability. The fetal period (week 9-birth) features growth and refinement of structures. Critical periods are windows during which specific experiences must occur for normal development — language acquisition has a critical period ending around puberty (evidence: Genie case). Infants are born with reflexes: rooting, sucking, grasping, and the Moro (startle) reflex.

core_concepts.title: Piaget's Cognitive Development Theory
core_concepts.summary: Jean Piaget proposed that cognitive development proceeds through four qualitatively distinct stages driven by assimilation (fitting new experiences into existing schemas) and accommodation (changing schemas to fit new experiences). Sensorimotor (0-2 years): object permanence develops by 8 months. Preoperational (2-7): language develops but egocentrism, animism, and inability to conserve persist. Concrete operational (7-12): logical operations on concrete objects; conservation, reversibility, seriation emerge; egocentrism decreases. Formal operational (12+): abstract hypothetical reasoning. Vygotsky's sociocultural view: the zone of proximal development (ZPD) — the gap between what a child can do alone vs. with guidance — and scaffolding (support that is gradually removed).

core_concepts.title: Attachment Theory
core_concepts.summary: Harry Harlow's rhesus monkey experiments showed that infant monkeys preferred a cloth surrogate mother over a wire one with food — demonstrating that contact comfort, not feeding, is critical for attachment. Lorenz showed imprinting in geese — early following behavior programmed during a critical period. Mary Ainsworth's Strange Situation identified attachment patterns: secure attachment (confident exploration, mild distress at separation, quickly comforted upon return), anxious-ambivalent, and avoidant. Securely attached infants have sensitive, responsive caregivers. Securely attached children develop better social and cognitive outcomes. Bowlby proposed that early attachment provides an internal working model for future relationships.

core_concepts.title: Social Development: Erikson's Stages and Parenting
core_concepts.summary: Erik Erikson proposed eight psychosocial stages across the lifespan, each defined by a conflict. Key stages: Trust vs. Mistrust (infancy), Autonomy vs. Shame (toddler), Initiative vs. Guilt (preschool), Industry vs. Inferiority (school age), Identity vs. Role Confusion (adolescence — most tested), Intimacy vs. Isolation (young adult), Generativity vs. Stagnation (middle adult), Integrity vs. Despair (older adult). Diana Baumrind identified three parenting styles: authoritative (warm + firm = best outcomes), authoritarian (firm, low warmth), permissive (warm, low firm). Peer relationships and social comparison become dominant influences in adolescence.

core_concepts.title: Moral Development and Adolescence
core_concepts.summary: Lawrence Kohlberg proposed three levels of moral reasoning: preconventional (self-interest and avoiding punishment), conventional (social rules and law and order), and postconventional (abstract principles and social contracts) — based on his Heinz dilemma studies. Carol Gilligan criticized Kohlberg for gender bias, noting women tend to reason from an ethics-of-care perspective. Adolescence involves puberty (primary and secondary sex characteristics), identity formation (Erikson's identity vs. role confusion), imaginary audience (believing others are watching you) and personal fable (believing oneself to be unique and invincible) — Elkind's concepts of adolescent egocentrism. Research shows that prefrontal cortex development is not complete until the mid-20s, explaining adolescent risk-taking.

key_terms.definition: Environmental agent (drug, pathogen, radiation) that can cause prenatal developmental damage
Pattern of physical and cognitive defects caused by maternal alcohol use during pregnancy
Time window during which specific experiences must occur for normal development (e.g., language acquisition)
Mental framework that organizes and interprets information
Interpreting new experiences through existing schemas
Modifying existing schemas or creating new ones to fit new experiences
Understanding that objects continue to exist when out of sight — emerges around 8 months
Understanding that quantity remains the same despite changes in shape or arrangement — develops in concrete operational stage
Piaget's term for preoperational children's inability to take another's perspective
Vygotsky's term for the gap between what a child can do alone and with guidance
Vygotsky's term for temporary support that enables learning in the ZPD
Harlow's finding that physical contact (not feeding) is the basis of infant attachment
Lorenz's term for early-life attachment formed through exposure during a critical period
Ainsworth attachment pattern: confident exploration, protest at separation, quickly soothed upon return
Attachment marked by separation anxiety and difficulty being soothed — inconsistent caregiving
Attachment marked by indifference to caregiver — associated with emotionally unavailable caregivers
Erikson's first stage (infancy): develops trust if caregivers are reliable; mistrust if neglectful
Erikson's adolescent stage: exploring and committing to a personal identity
Baumrind's warm + firm parenting style associated with best child outcomes
Kohlberg's first level: moral reasoning based on self-interest and avoiding punishment
Kohlberg's second level: conforming to social rules and laws to maintain social order
Kohlberg's third level: abstract principles (justice, human rights) that may transcend laws
Elkind's term: adolescent belief that others are constantly watching and judging them
Elkind's term: adolescent belief in their own uniqueness and invincibility
Ability to understand that others have beliefs, desires, and perspectives different from one's own — develops around age 4

exam_tip: Piaget's four stages appear on nearly every AP Psychology exam. Know the key achievement of each stage: sensorimotor (object permanence), preoperational (symbolic thought, egocentrism, NO conservation), concrete operational (conservation, logical reasoning on concrete objects), formal operational (abstract/hypothetical reasoning). For Erikson, focus on adolescence (Identity vs. Role Confusion) — but know the infant stage (Trust vs. Mistrust) and later stages too. Harlow (contact comfort vs. feeding) and Ainsworth (secure/insecure attachment) appear frequently in stimulus-based questions.
